ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper applies and adapts the idea of learned optimizers (LSTM-based update rules) to the few-shot/meta-learning setting and additionally trains a task-common initialization for rapid adaptation — a combination and focus that is not present in the cited prior work. It properly acknowledges close antecedents (Andrychowicz et al. 2016, Hochreiter et al., Bertinetto et al., Matching Networks) and clearly differentiates its contribution as tailoring a learned-LSTM optimizer to k-shot, N-class episodes and matching train/test conditions. This is a notable and non-trivial extension (novel application + learned initialization) rather than a wholly new foundational idea, so it merits a high but not maximal originality score.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well-organized and follows a clear structure (Introduction → Task → Model → Experiments → Conclusion) and states its main contributions early (an LSTM-based meta-learner that learns updates and an initialization). Notation is largely consistent and key equations (Eq. 1–2, gate definitions, preprocessing) are defined, but the presentation suffers from minor clarity issues: a few typographical errors (e.g. “neutral networks”), some duplicated/extraneous text at the end of the provided extract, and references to figures/tables that are not present in the excerpt, which impairs readability. Overall the writing is good and understandable, with only modest presentation problems to fix.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper clearly states its main claim (an LSTM meta-learner can learn an initialization and update rule that yields good few-shot performance) and provides experiments on a Mini-ImageNet benchmark and relevant baselines that generally support this claim. The key mathematical mapping (gradient-descent update to LSTM cell update, Equations 1–2) is correct and there are no formal derivation errors; heavy theory/proofs are not required for the work presented. The experimental methodology is mostly sound (appropriate baselines, description of learner/meta-learner architectures, preprocessing and training protocol), but comparability to prior work is weakened by using a custom Mini-ImageNet split and by implementing Matching Networks themselves (possible implementation mismatches) and the paper does not present detailed statistical tests beyond stating confidence intervals. A notable simplifying assumption (ignoring meta-gradient dependence / second derivatives) is acknowledged but not theoretically justified, though the authors report it works empirically. Overall the conclusions are supported within the experimental scope, with only minor methodological caveats.
